segments.speaker: 题目8/9/10/11
segments.text: Listen to a talk on a science podcast. Nanoparticles are particles whose size is less than 1:10 thousandth of a millimeter. Super tiny nanoparticles have numerous applications. My family had a picnic last Saturday. We used sunscreen to avoid sunburn. Our sunscreen contains titanium dioxide, which has been used in screen for decades. You used to end up with these horble looking White smears all over your skin while wearing it. But our new sunscreen's titanium dioxide, comes in nanoparticcles. They block ultraviolet sunlight, but are transparent. The sun block's completely invisible on your skin, not smeary White. Another example, the metal silver has antimicrobial properties. The problem is you can't put regular metal on a wound, but in ointment. With silver nanoparticles, we can actually apply that to stop bacterial growth. We did it when my son scraped his knee at the picnic. Humans have actually used nanoparticles for millennia. Take ancient Romes tesaray tesaray are tiny bits of highly colorful glass that the Romans arranged to create various images. Many Roman tesseray, especially pink ones, contain gold nanoparticcles. The particles affect how light interacts with the glass and create the color. Did the Romans know what they were doing? Well, they probably didn't understand the physics of it, but some glass workshop could realize that gold foil beaten to an extremely thin layer produces bright colors when heated between glass layers. The technique was perfected and spread. All pink test array contain gold nanoparticles at between ten and 30 parts per million. That's so consistent. So it's definitely not random.
